sanskrit_devanagari:
नास्ति बुद्धिरयुक्तस्य न चायुक्तस्य भावना ।
न चाभावयतः शान्तिरशान्तस्य कुतः सुखम् ॥ ६६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
nāsti buddhir ayuktasya na cāyuktasya bhāvanā |
na cābhāvayataḥ śāntir aśāntasya kutaḥ sukham || 66 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; uses single danda | as terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.66।।)
IAST exact (Devanagari rendered without transliteration marks; IAST body matches: 'nasti buddhir ayuktasya na cayuktasya bhavana na cabhavayatah santir asantasya kutah sukham')
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.066 = MBh_06,024.066). Body text identical: 'nāsti buddhir ayuktasya na cāyuktasya bhāvanā / na cābhāvayataḥ śāntir aśāntasya kutaḥ sukham'.

anvaya.meaning: of the unyoked / of the undisciplined
of the non-contemplative / of one not engaged in contemplation
of the unpeaceful / of one without peace

translations.verbatim_quote: One who is not connected with the Supreme [in Kṛṣṇa consciousness] can have neither transcendental intelligence nor a steady mind, without which there is no possibility of peace. And how can there be any happiness without peace?

But an undisciplined person, who has not controlled the mind and senses, can neither have a resolute intellect nor steady contemplation on God. For one who never unites the mind with God there is no peace; and how can one who lacks peace be happy?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Unless one is in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, there is no possibility of peace. So it is confirmed in the Fifth Chapter that when one is actually in Kṛṣṇa consciousness he is then in real peace. Therefore, complete Kṛṣṇa consciousness is the platform of peace and happiness.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Shankara bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Ayuktasya, for the unsteady, for one who does not have a concentrated mind; na asti, there is no, i.e. there does not arise; buddhih, wisdom, with regard to the nature of the Self; ca, and; there is no bhavana, meditation, earnest longing for the knowledge of the Self; ayuktasya, for an unsteady man. And similarly, abhavayatah, for an unmeditative man, who does not ardently desire the knowledge of the Self; there is no santih, peace, rest of the senses; asantasya, for the unpeaceful man; kutah, where is; sukham, happiness?
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as a four-step negative chain. For the unyoked (ayukta — without a concentrated mind): no buddhi (wisdom about the Self) and no bhāvanā (sustained meditation, earnest longing for self-knowledge). For the non-contemplative (abhāvayataḥ): no peace (śānti — rest of the senses). For the unpeaceful (aśānta): the rhetorical question — where is happiness? The chain is sequential and negative: each absence produces the next absence. Shankara's gloss makes the structural claim explicit: there is no shortcut. Without yoga (concentrated mind) there is no wisdom; without wisdom there is no contemplation; without contemplation there is no peace; without peace there is no happiness.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (short excerpt of translator-rendered Ramanuja bhashya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In him who does not focus his mind on Me but is engaged only in the control of senses by his own exertion, the Buddhi or the right disposition that is concerned with the pure self never arises. Therefore he fails in the practice of meditation on the self. In one who cannot think of the pure self, there arises the desire for sense objects; in him serenity does not arise. How can eternal and unsurpassed bliss be generated in him who is not serene but is attached to sense-objects?
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the verse as the closure of the chain begun in 2.61. The unyoked one — specifically, one who tries to control the senses by sheer will without focussing the mind on the Supreme — never produces buddhi about the pure self, hence cannot meditate, hence cannot be serene, hence cannot have lasting bliss. Ramanuja's reading sharpens the negative chain into a critique: bare sense-control without the meditative focus on the Supreme is the failure mode the verse is naming. The bare-will path is not a shortcut to peace; it is the absence of the precondition for peace.

positions.rendering: one who is not connected with the Supreme [in Kṛṣṇa consciousness]
an undisciplined person, who has not controlled the mind and senses
the unsteady, one who does not have a concentrated mind
one who does not focus his mind on Me but is engaged only in the control of senses by his own exertion

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: ayukta literally means 'unyoked, undisciplined, not joined' — the negation of yukta (yoked, joined, disciplined) in 2.61. The four readings differ on what ayukta is the negation of. Shankara reads it generically as 'not having a concentrated mind' (Advaita yoga). Ramanuja reads it specifically as the failure-mode of bare-will sense-control without meditative focus on the Supreme. Prabhupada reads it as 'not connected with Kṛṣṇa consciousness' (Vaishnava devotion). Mukundananda renders the surface-level 'undisciplined'. The verse text alone underdetermines which reading is primary; the chain back to 2.61's mat-paraḥ supports Ramanuja's reading lexically, but Shankara's reading is also internally consistent.

positions.rendering: where (could there be) happiness? (rhetorical question expecting 'nowhere')

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: There is no translator disagreement on kutaḥ sukham. The phrase is rhetorically forceful — a question that closes the verse by asserting the impossibility of happiness for the unpeaceful. The Sanskrit construction (kutaḥ + nominative) is a standard rhetorical device in Sanskrit philosophical literature meaning 'how could there possibly be X?'. The verse closes the section's negative chain not as a soft observation but as a strong claim: happiness is structurally impossible without peace, and peace is structurally impossible without contemplation, and so on backward.

literal_meaning: There is no intelligence for the unyoked, and no contemplation for the unyoked; for the non-contemplative there is no peace; for one without peace, where is happiness?
traditional_meaning_consensus: This verse closes the sthita-prajña block's middle section with a four-step negative chain. The unyoked (ayukta) has no buddhi (wisdom). The unyoked has no bhāvanā (sustained contemplation). The non-contemplative (abhāvayataḥ) has no śānti (peace). And for the unpeaceful (aśānta), happiness is structurally impossible — kutaḥ sukham? The rhetorical question is forceful, not soft. Shankara reads the chain in Advaita yoga terms (concentrated mind → wisdom → contemplation → peace → happiness). Ramanuja reads it as the closure of the chain begun in 2.61: bare sense-control without focus on the Supreme is the failure mode the verse names. Gaudiya Vaishnava reading folds the chain into Krishna-consciousness as the precondition. All traditions agree: there is no shortcut. The engineer who tries to skip the attention-discipline and 'just be productive' produces worse work; the verse closes this section by making the structural claim that happiness has preconditions, and those preconditions are not optional.

remaining_gaps: Shankara's full Sanskrit bhashya not pulled — only translator-rendered (Gambhirananda) excerpt was machine-extractable.
